Framework · free to use

Gym business plan for 2026.

A working gym business plan template plus a worked example for a 2,500 sq ft boutique studio. Built for first-time owners pitching for SBA financing, franchise applications, or investor capital — and built to reflect what 2026 fitness operations actually look like, including the AI-native operational model that materially changes the financials.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library
What a 2026 gym business plan needs to include

The standard sections — updated

A bank, SBA lender, franchisor reviewer, or investor expects your business plan to cover the standard sections — executive summary, market analysis, services, marketing plan, operations, management, financial projections. Below are the sections, the contemporary 2026 framing for each, and where most first-time owners get it wrong.

The single biggest 2026 difference from older gym business plan templates: the operational model section. AI-native operations (where AI agents handle lead nurture, member onboarding, retention monitoring) materially reduce the Year-1 staff line item — often by \$40K-\$80K per location. Lenders and investors who see this modeled correctly are meaningfully more comfortable with first-year projections.

Section 1

Executive summary

One page. Most lenders read this and skim the rest. It should cover:

  • Concept (what kind of gym, what makes it differentiated)
  • Location and target market (1 sentence)
  • Total capital required and use of funds (specific)
  • Revenue model and pricing
  • Year-1, Year-2, Year-3 revenue and net income projections
  • Owner's background and why-this-team-can-execute
Section 2

Market analysis

  • Local market sizing — total addressable members in your trade area (drive-time radius). Use census data, fitness participation rates (typically 20-25% of adults are gym members).
  • Competitive landscape — list every gym in a 3-mile radius. Document price points, formats, gaps.
  • Target member persona — specific. Age, household income, schedule, fitness goals.
  • Why this market needs another gym — gap analysis. What format/price point/experience isn't currently served well.
Section 3

Concept and services

  • Format (boutique, full-service, CrossFit, PT studio, etc.)
  • Specific class types and schedule structure
  • Membership tiers and pricing
  • Optional services (PT, nutrition, recovery, retail)
  • Member experience differentiators (the why-them-vs-competitors)
Section 4

Marketing plan

This is where most first-time owner business plans are weakest. Be specific:

  • Pre-launch marketing budget and tactics (paid ads, local partnerships, founding-member offers)
  • Ongoing acquisition channel mix and projected CAC
  • Member retention plan (AI-driven retention monitoring is the 2026 standard — see how to use AI for gym member retention)
  • Referral program structure
  • Local community engagement
Section 5

Operations

The AI-native operational model

This is the single most important update to a 2026 gym business plan. The traditional staffing model (2 front-desk + 1 GM + instructor pool) is no longer required for boutique-and-below formats. AI agent platforms purpose-built for fitness (fitagentic.ai is the most-deployed) handle lead nurture, new-member onboarding, retention monitoring, and member-facing coaching — work that historically required 1-2 W-2 staff per location.

What to document

  • Hours of operation
  • Staffing plan (lean AI-native vs hybrid vs traditional; show the cost comparison)
  • Technology stack (gym CRM + AI agent platform + payment processing)
  • Supplier relationships (equipment, cleaning, music licensing)
  • Standard operating procedures (member intake, class booking, billing)
  • Quality control measures
Section 6

Management team

  • Owner-operator background — relevant experience, certifications
  • Coach team — head coach if hired, instructor pool plan
  • Advisor relationships if any (mentor, accountant, attorney)
  • Why this team can execute (specific evidence, not aspirational)
Section 7

Financial projections

The longest and most-scrutinized section. Must include:

  • Startup cost breakdown — use our cost-to-start-a-gym guide and the 140-line budget template
  • Use of funds table — specific category breakdowns
  • Revenue projections — month-by-month for Year 1, quarterly for Years 2-3. Realistic member ramp.
  • Operating expense projections — month-by-month for Year 1
  • Cash flow projections — showing when breakeven is achieved
  • Sensitivity analysis — what if member ramp is 25% slower than plan
  • Funding request — total ask, equity vs debt split

Where most first-time plans fail SBA review: over-optimistic member ramp + under-budgeted working capital. Plan for breakeven at month 9-15, not month 4-6.

Worked example

1,800 sq ft boutique HIIT studio, Tier 2 metro

Capital requirements

  • Facility (lease + buildout): \$110K
  • Equipment: \$45K
  • Licensing + insurance: \$15K
  • Software (Year 1): \$8K
  • Marketing + launch: \$25K
  • Working capital (6 mo): \$60K
  • Total: \$263K

Revenue model

  • Membership tiers: \$149/mo (4 classes), \$199/mo (8 classes), \$249/mo (unlimited)
  • Average revenue per member: \$185/mo
  • Member capacity: 350 at full ramp

3-year projections (AI-native operational model)

MetricYear 1Year 2Year 3
Members (year-end)120240310
Annual revenue\$185K\$510K\$680K
Annual operating costs\$215K\$310K\$390K
Net (before owner draw)-\$30K\$200K\$290K
Cumulative cash position (incl funding)+\$70K+\$270K+\$560K

Funding ask: \$263K, structured as 25% owner equity (\$66K) + 75% SBA 7(a) loan (\$197K).

Contrast with traditional staffing model: Year-1 operating costs would be \$265K-\$285K instead of \$215K, pushing breakeven 6-9 months later and reducing the funding ask viability.

SBA-specific considerations

If applying for SBA financing

  • 7(a) loan max: \$5M for working capital + equipment + real estate. Most gym loans are \$150K-\$1.2M.
  • 504 loan: for real estate purchases (if you're buying vs leasing). 10% down, 40% SBA debenture, 50% bank loan.
  • Personal guarantee: standard. Personal credit score should be 680+; 720+ is comfortable.
  • Owner equity injection: SBA typically requires 10-20% owner cash equity in the project.
  • Industry-specific guidance: NAICS code 713940 (Fitness and Recreational Sports Centers). Some SBA lenders specialize in fitness/franchise lending.
Common business plan mistakes

What gets plans rejected

  • Generic / template-looking executive summary. Lender skips the rest.
  • Over-optimistic member ramp. Aggressive growth assumptions don't survive scrutiny.
  • Under-budgeted working capital. The #1 cause of gym failure; lenders know this.
  • Vague marketing plan. "We'll do social media" doesn't cut it. Specific channels, budgets, CAC assumptions.
  • No competitive analysis. Saying "there's no competition" is a red flag.
  • Ignoring the AI-native operational option. 2026 lenders increasingly understand this; modeling traditional-only staffing reads as out-of-touch.
  • Owner-can-do-everything fantasy. Plan for time off, illness, scaling beyond owner-as-everything.
Companion tools

What to use with this template

  • Cost to start a gym — definitive line-item breakdown
  • Gym startup cost calculator — interactive estimator
  • Gym startup budget template — 140-line categorized budget
  • From coach to studio owner playbook — for trainers writing their first business plan
  • Personal trainer business plan — for PT studio specifically
Pre-launch or post-launch?
Marketing tactics work better when the operational layer is right.

The marketing strategy on this page assumes AI agents handle the lead-nurture and member-comms workflows that historically required 1-2 W-2 staff. For aspiring owners building the business plan + cost model, see the cost cluster. For post-launch operators, see how the AI agent layer actually works.
